one_line_takeaway: CSSBench shows that Chinese-specific surface obfuscation (pinyin, homophones, symbol-mixing, zero-width chars) reliably breaks the guardrails of lightweight Chinese LLMs — leaving ~1 in 3 obfuscated harmful prompts answered — and that the models which look safest on clean prompts degrade the most under these attacks, with much apparent safety being blanket over-refusal.
key_finding: Lightweight Chinese LLMs are broadly vulnerable to Chinese-specific obfuscation: overall CER ranges ~30.6 to 47.4, and ASR on obfuscated malicious queries stays roughly 31-39, i.e. about one in three obfuscated harmful prompts still elicits unsafe content even with default guardrails on. Qwen3-8B is safest (CER ~30.6, overall ASR 30.58%); MiniCPM4-0.5B is worst (CER ~47.4, ASR 47.38%); Pangu-1B is the best sub-2B model. Critically, apparent safety often comes from over-refusal rather than genuine robustness: several models show high ORR (blanket rejection) while ASR stays high. The ablation reveals an inversion — the models that look safest on clean prompts degrade the MOST under adversarial patterns (Qwen3-8B average ASR increase of +48% across domains and +60% across task types; Qwen3-1.7B +45%/+40%), whereas already-weak models (Hunyuan family) show near-zero or negative change because their clean-prompt ASR is already saturated. By domain, adult content and fraud/hate are most exploitable (ASR often >50%) while health/medical misinformation and privacy leakage are better protected (<20% for several models); public/political safety stays hard (>30% even for the best). By task format, open-ended QA is most vulnerable (~45-65%), MCQ moderate (~30%), and TF judgment safest.
core_contribution: CSSBench, a new Chinese-specific safety benchmark for evaluating lightweight (<=8B) LLMs against surface-form adversarial obfuscation that preserves harmful intent. The paper's own contribution is (1) a dataset spanning six safety domains (~150 instances/domain) organized into three task formats (MCQ, true-false, open-ended QA); (2) construction of adversarial variants of each malicious query using four Chinese-specific obfuscation patterns — Pinyin Mix, Homophone/visual substitution, Symbol Mix (emoji/Latin/digit/split), and Zero-width character insertion — that alter orthography without changing semantics; (3) a Chinese-native over-refusal set of 250 borderline benign queries (built from XSTest templates rewritten by Gemini-3, then manually filtered) to capture culturally specific refusal triggers; and (4) a composite error rate (CER) metric that micro-averages unsafe compliance on malicious queries and refusals on benign queries into a single harmlessness/helpfulness trade-off score. Table 1 positions CSSBench as the only benchmark jointly covering all four task formats, all listed adversarial patterns, and over-refusal, versus prior Chinese benchmarks (ChineseSafe, SafetyBench, JailBench, FLAMES, FORTRESS, Chinese Safeguards, ToxiCloakCN).
threat_model: An adversary issues Chinese-language malicious queries whose harmful intent is concealed through language-specific surface transformations — pinyin/abbreviated pinyin romanization, near-homophone or shape-similar/traditional-character substitution, mixed-script insertion of Latin letters/digits/emoji with symbol-based splitting, and invisible zero-width character insertion (e.g., U+200B). These perturbations keep the meaning recoverable to a human Chinese reader while defeating character-based detectors, token-level keyword filters, and literal string matching. The paper targets default safety behavior of deployed lightweight/on-device Chinese LLMs (no added safety system prompt) and measures both unsafe compliance (jailbreak success) and the dual failure of over-refusal on benign borderline inputs.
limitations: Authors state the benchmark is a focused complement, not a replacement, for broader safety evaluation. It deliberately over-samples high-difficulty obfuscated queries and contains relatively few naive/clean harmful prompts, so it does not characterize behavior on straightforward prompts or generic multilingual tests. It fixes six domains and three task formats (not exhaustive of harms/interaction patterns); the adversarial pattern set is curated and finite and cannot exhaust evolving real-world obfuscation; the empirical study is limited to ten sub-8B Chinese models, so findings should not be generalized to larger or future models; and the benchmark is text-only and Mandarin-centric, ignoring multimodal input, dialects, and code-switching. Additional unstated caveats: reliance on a single automated judge (Qwen3Guard) that may carry its own biases (mitigated only by manual spot-checks), and over-refusal borderline data partly synthesized by Gemini-3.

datasets_used: CSSBench (this paper's own benchmark): six safety domains ~150 instances/domain — illegal activities and compliance, privacy leakage, health and medical misinformation, fraud and hate, adult content, public and political safety
CSSBench over-refusal subset: 250 borderline benign queries built from XSTest templates rewritten/expanded by Gemini-3 and manually filtered
Adversarial variants generated with four Chinese-specific patterns: Pinyin Mix, Homophone, Symbol Mix, Zero-width insertion
quantitative_results: Overall CER across models ranges ~30.6 to 47.4 (lower is better)
Pangu-1B best sub-2B model: overall ASR 35.71%
Overall ASR values (Table 2): Pangu-7B 34.50%, MiniCPM4-8B 39.61%, Hunyuan-1.8B 41.11%, Qwen3-1.7B 41.55%, Hunyuan-0.5B 44.34%, Hunyuan-7B 44.69%, Qwen3-0.6B 44.99%
ASR on obfuscated queries stays roughly 31-39 across models; ~1 in 3 obfuscated malicious prompts still answered unsafely

Domain ASR: adult content and fraud/hate often >50% (e.g. MiniCPM4-8B adult content 77.33%, Qwen3-8B adult content 76.00%); health/medical misinformation and privacy leakage <20% for several models (Qwen3-8B privacy leakage 5.33%, health/medical 14.00%)
Task-format ASR: open-ended QA highest ~45-65%, MCQ ~30%, TF lowest
Adversarial-vs-clean ablation (domain avg change): Qwen3-8B +48%, Qwen3-1.7B +45%, Pangu-7B +30%, Pangu-1B +26%; Hunyuan-1.8B -6%, Hunyuan-7B -7%, MiniCPM4-0.5B -1%
Adversarial-vs-clean ablation (task-type avg change): Qwen3-8B +60% (MCQ +22%, QA +71%, TF +85%), Qwen3-1.7B +40%, Pangu-1B +28%, Pangu-7B +22%; Hunyuan-1.8B -6%
baselines_compared: Models under test (10 lightweight LLMs <=8B): Qwen3-0.6B, Qwen3-1.7B, Qwen3-8B, MiniCPM4-0.5B, MiniCPM4-8B, Hunyuan-0.5B, Hunyuan-1.8B, Hunyuan-7B, openPangu-Embedded-1B, openPangu-Embedded-7B
Prior Chinese safety benchmarks compared for coverage (Table 1): ChineseSafe, SafetyBench, JailBench, FLAMES, FORTRESS, Chinese Safeguards, ToxiCloakCN
